text: The electrochemical oxidation of 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) to 2,5-furandicarboxylic acid (FDCA) represents a sustainable pathway for producing bio-based polymers, yet the process is frequently complicated by the inherent instability of HMF in the alkaline media required for efficient catalysis. In this study, we investigate the alkaline degradation of HMF and demonstrate that it undergoes a selective Cannizzaro disproportionation, forming 5-hydroxymethyl-2-furancarboxylic acid (HMFCA) and dihydroxymethylfuran (DHMF). Unlike HMF, which readily polymerizes into insoluble humins at high pH, these Cannizzaro products remain stable in strongly alkaline solutions even at elevated temperatures. We show that both HMFCA and DHMF can be effectively electrooxidized to FDCA with high faradaic efficiencies (~98%) using transition-metal-based electrodes. By leveraging the stability of these intermediates, we developed an "indirect" electrochemical route that enables the processing of HMF at significantly higher substrate and base concentrations than previously reported. This approach mitigates humin formation and allows for technical-scale current densities exceeding 1 A cm⁻², substantially enhancing the space–time yield and commercial viability of biomass-derived plastic precursor production.
